The chain-length composition of hotspot sediments directly influences this temporal dimension, with long-chain compounds exhibiting slower release kinetics that prolong secondary source activity. Additionally, understanding hotspot formation and secondary source potential provides essential context for interpreting groundwater concentration baselines, particularly in distinguishing true background conditions from legacy contamination signals. Elevated aquifer PFAS concentrations in areas lacking identifiable current sources frequently reflect sediment hotspot reactivation rather than undetected ongoing discharge—a consideration central to establishing defensible groundwater concentration baselines. Furthermore, this recognition shifts monitoring priorities toward sediment characterization and hyporheic zone assessment in groundwater protection strategies. The formation mechanisms underlying hotspot development reveal critical dependencies on sediment physicochemical properties and hydrodynamic conditions that govern PFAS partitioning dynamics. Fine-grained sediment fractions, particularly those rich in organic carbon and iron oxyhydroxides, provide preferential binding sites for long-chain PFAS compounds, concentrating contamination in discrete stratigraphic horizons [10]. However, this grain-size selectivity creates a heterogeneous distribution where contaminant mass concentrates in thin, high-capacity layers rather than distributing uniformly through the sediment column, amplifying local concentration gradients driving diffusive flux to porewater. The hydraulic connectivity between hotspot sediments and underlying aquifers operates through multiple transport regimes that vary substantially with seasonal and climatic conditions. Moreover, during high-flow periods, advective transport through surface-connected preferential flow paths dominates mass transfer, delivering concentrated PFAS pulses to groundwater systems [10]. Low-flow conditions shift dominance toward diffusive exchange across the sediment-water interface, sustaining chronic baseline loading at reduced rates. This regime-dependent transport variability complicates predictive modeling of aquifer loading rates, as empirical measurements at single time points may poorly characterize annual mass loading budgets. Furthermore, the competitive displacement dynamics within hotspots warrant particular analytical attention. Co-occurring inorganic and organic contaminants, including natural organic matter and competing anions, can trigger episodic PFAS release through competitive sorption mechanisms [10]. Moreover, these displacement events, often associated with seasonal organic matter fluxes or anthropogenic chemical inputs, generate transient concentration spikes in porewater that propagate rapidly through connected groundwater pathways. The magnitude of competitive release scales inversely with perfluorinated chain length, reflecting weaker binding affinities for short-chain compounds that render them more susceptible to displacement from sediment binding sites. Temperature fluctuations introduce an additional dimension of hotspot reactivation potential. Nevertheless, elevated temperatures reduce sorption equilibrium constants for PFAS compounds, particularly for longer-chain homologues where hydrophobic interactions contribute substantially to binding free energy. Climate warming scenarios thus project enhanced secondary source activity from existing sediment reservoirs, with thermal sensitivity varying systematically across the PFAS chain-length spectrum. In addition, this temperature-dependent release mechanism intersects with seasonal thermal stratification in surface water bodies, creating depth-variable reactivation patterns within sediment profiles. The spatial configuration of hotspots relative to groundwater extraction infrastructure determines practical exposure consequences. Hotspots positioned within capture zones of municipal or agricultural wells generate sustained contaminant breakthrough curves characterized by extended tailing due to rate-limited desorption kinetics. However, this temporal dispersion contrasts with more immediate contamination signals from primary source proximity, complicating source attribution in groundwater quality investigations. The diagnostic differentiation between ongoing primary discharge and reactivated secondary loading requires multi-tracer approaches incorporating compound-specific isotope analysis and co-contaminant fingerprinting. In addition, sediment hotspot longevity presents fundamental challenges for groundwater remediation timeline planning. The substantial PFAS mass inventories accumulated in near-surface sediments, combined with slow release kinetics, establish multi-decadal contamination trajectories that extend substantially beyond conventional groundwater remediation timeframes. This persistence necessitates adaptive management frameworks incorporating sediment hotspot treatment or containment as integral components of comprehensive aquifer restoration strategies, rather than relying exclusively on groundwater extraction and treatment approaches that address symptoms without eliminating secondary sources. Additionally, the air-water interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS compounds introduces a critical dimension to hotspot formation dynamics that complements sediment-sorbed reservoirs. In unsaturated soil zones and hyporheic environments where air-water interfaces abound, long-chain compounds partition preferentially into interfacial regions, creating secondary concentration gradients that parallel their sediment-bound hotspots. In addition, this interfacial partitioning effectively sequesters long-chain PFAS in transient, mobile reservoirs distinct from sediment binding sites, generating a dual-reservoir system where contamination exists in both sorbed and interfacial phases. These findings establish that soil concentration measurements alone provide insufficient proxies for groundwater loading potential, as the magnitude of porewater mobilization depends on dynamic equilibrium conditions rather than total mass inventories. Field-scale quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water systems remains limited, with existing studies addressing this methodological gap through integrated monitoring approaches that capture bidirectional flux dynamics. In addition, this biological endpoint-based categorization aligns with physicochemical partitioning patterns, as compounds exhibiting greater bioaccumulation concurrently demonstrate stronger environmental retention and more pronounced hotspot formation. The convergence of biological and geochemical classification schemes supports predictive risk assessment frameworks that translate molecular structure into environmental fate projections. Additionally, the air-water interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS compounds introduces a critical dimension to hotspot formation dynamics that complements sediment-sorbed reservoirs. This interfacial partitioning effectively sequesters long-chain PFAS in transient, mobile reservoirs distinct from sediment binding sites, generating a dual-reservoir system where contamination exists in both sorbed and interfacial phases. These findings establish that soil concentration measurements alone provide insufficient proxies for groundwater loading potential, as the magnitude of porewater mobilization depends on dynamic equilibrium conditions rather than total mass inventories. In addition, this biological endpoint-based categorization aligns with physicochemical partitioning patterns, as compounds exhibiting greater bioaccumulation concurrently demonstrate stronger environmental retention and more pronounced hotspot formation. The convergence of biological and geochemical classification schemes supports predictive risk assessment frameworks that translate molecular structure into environmental fate projections. As a result, PFAS are commonly known as 'forever chemicals' because they do not readily degrade in nature, and are known as forever chemicals because they are stable and persistent in the environment and in the human body, meaning they do not break down and can accumulate over time. PFAS are constantly spreading in the environment and have been found in water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and humans across the nation. Field-scale quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water systems remains limited, with existing studies addressing this methodological gap through integrated monitoring approaches that capture bidirectional flux dynamics.\n\n## Groundwater-Surface Water Exchange and Mass Transfer Dynamics\n\nThe groundwater-surface water interface constitutes the critical boundary where subsurface PFAS plumes encounter hydrologically connected receptors, yet quantitative characterization of mass transfer across this transition remains substantially incomplete. Additionally, this methodological limitation constrains predictive capabilities for contaminant fate modeling, as field-validated mass transfer coefficients for PFAS compounds remain scarce. In addition, the hyporheic zone serves as the primary locus for bidirectional PFAS flux between groundwater and surface water systems. Hydrological gradients drive advective transport across this interface, while concentration gradients established through differential partitioning mechanisms govern diffusive components. The relative dominance of advection versus diffusion depends on aquifer hydraulic conductivity, stream stage variability, and head differentials between surface and subsurface systems. Moreover, long-chain compounds exhibit pronounced affinity for sedimentary organic carbon, creating concentration discontinuities at the groundwater-surface water transition. Sorptive retention dampens instantaneous flux into surface waters but establishes sustained release conditions where contaminated groundwater discharge occurs over extended timescales, a dynamic elaborated in Section [X.X] regarding soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms in unsaturated zones. Moreover, short-chain PFAS demonstrate weaker sediment binding, facilitating more rapid equilibration across the interface. This reduced retardation enables higher instantaneous mass flux rates per unit concentration gradient, fundamentally altering the temporal dynamics of contaminant delivery to surface receptors. For nationwide distribution patterns of short-chain versus long-chain compound mobility, see Section [X.X]. In addition, temperature and pH fluctuations at the interface modulate sorption equilibrium and transient mass transfer rates, as detailed in Section [X.X] regarding soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms. Seasonal groundwater temperature variations alter PFAS aqueous solubility and sorption partition coefficients, introducing temporal dynamics that complicate steady-state mass balance calculations. Moreover, episodic precipitation events generate hydraulic transients that temporarily reverse flow directions across the hyporheic zone, inducing bidirectional contaminant migration that challenges unidirectional transport assumptions in standard exposure models. Surface water discharge fundamentally transforms contaminant fate pathways. Mass entering surface waters becomes subject to more rapid advective distribution across receptor exposure zones compared to groundwater-contained mass. Furthermore, photolytic and biotic transformation processes absent in groundwater may alter PFAS profiles, with the relative persistence of long-chain versus short-chain compounds determining whether surface water discharge represents a terminal sink or temporary transfer mechanism. Treatment system design requires explicit consideration of chain-length-dependent mass transfer kinetics. Additionally, extraction well networks positioned to capture groundwater before surface water discharge must account for the enhanced mobility of short-chain PFAS, which may bypass capture zones designed for strongly retarded long-chain compounds. In-situ treatment barriers face kinetic limitations when short-chain PFAS transit times through reactive media prove insufficient for complete degradation or sorption. The bidirectional nature of hyporheic exchange carries additional complexity for remedial decision-making. In addition, surface water infiltration during high-flow conditions transports PFAS into shallow aquifer zones, effectively expanding the subsurface contamination footprint beyond original source delineations. Coupled with sediment re-suspension dynamics, this creates feedback loops where surface water becomes a secondary source for groundwater contamination rather than solely a receptor. Additionally, quantifying these exchange dynamics through field instrumentation remains essential for parameterizing predictive models. Nested piezometer arrays, seepage meters, and differential flow gauging constitute critical measurement approaches. Current reliance on laboratory-derived partition coefficients and numerical approximations for dispersion characteristics introduces uncertainty that propagates through risk assessment calculations. Additionally, enhanced field characterization targeting chain-length-specific mass transfer rates would substantially improve confidence in exposure forecasts and remedial performance predictions for groundwater-surface water systems impacted by PFAS contamination. Field-based quantification of PFAS mass transfer coefficients remains notably scarce, representing a persistent methodological gap that constrains model validation efforts. In addition, laboratory-derived sorption parameters frequently fail to capture the heterogeneity of natural hyporheic sediments, where organic carbon content varies spatially over orders of magnitude and mineral surface chemistry differs substantially from simplified experimental matrices. Mass transfer rates estimated from batch experiments may deviate from field-scale behavior by factors that remain uncharacterized for most PFAS compounds. The manufacturing facility context examined in recent field investigations demonstrates how source-area hydrogeology governs plume discharge characteristics to receiving waters. Furthermore, high-resolution temporal monitoring reveals that PFAS concentration profiles in discharging groundwater do not mirror static source compositions, indicating that subsurface transformation and differential transport fractionation occur prior to surface water entry. This finding carries significant implications for receptor exposure assessment, as surface water concentrations may poorly reflect original source signatures. Moreover, chain-length-dependent discharge patterns emerge distinctly under field conditions. Long-chain PFAS exhibit pronounced concentration attenuation across the hyporheic zone due to combined sorptive and diffusive limitations, whereas short-chain homologs demonstrate more conservative transport with mass flux rates correlating strongly with groundwater discharge velocity. The resulting compositional shift between groundwater and surface water end-members necessitates separate analytical frameworks for exposure modeling rather than simple dilution calculations. Moreover, seasonal hydrological variability introduces additional complexity for mass transfer quantification. Baseflow conditions favor sustained, low-concentration discharge of long-chain PFAS from sediment storage reservoirs, while stormflow events mobilize short-chain compounds through enhanced groundwater-surface water connectivity and reduced hydraulic residence times. Moreover, this temporal partitioning means that annual mass loading estimates based on single-season sampling campaigns risk substantial bias depending on which hydrological regime dominates the monitoring period. The bidirectional flux characterization presents particular challenges for remedial endpoint establishment. Surface water quality criteria typically assume continuous discharge scenarios, yet episodic infiltration during flood events can reverse contaminant migration directions and expand the effective source zone. Furthermore, remedial systems designed solely for groundwater capture may prove inadequate when surface water constitutes a secondary contamination pathway, requiring integrated monitoring approaches that track mass movement across both domains simultaneously. Analytical detection capabilities further constrain field-based mass transfer studies. In addition, the ultra-trace concentrations characteristic of PFAS in environmental matrices demand specialized extraction and quantification methods that introduce systematic biases between groundwater and surface water sample types. Method reporting limits that differ between matrices can create apparent concentration discontinuities that reflect analytical artifacts rather than true mass transfer behavior, complicating coefficient derivation from paired measurements. Future field investigation priorities should emphasize direct measurement of interfacial fluxes through nested instrumentation capable of resolving concentration gradients at centimeter scales within the hyporheic zone. Additionally, such approaches would enable validation of numerical model formulations currently reliant on assumed equilibrium conditions that may not prevail under dynamic field conditions. Air-water interfacial phenomena introduce additional complexity to PFAS mass transfer dynamics at the groundwater-surface water interface. In addition, this differential interfacial adsorption capacity fundamentally alters transport behavior in systems where entrapped air or fluctuating water tables create extensive air-water boundaries. Long-chain compounds may experience enhanced retardation in unsaturated zone transitions preceding surface water discharge, effectively decoupling their arrival times from groundwater flow velocities. The significance of air-water interfacial adsorption extends beyond unsaturated zone processes. In contrast, recent investigations demonstrated that this mechanism governs the distribution of longer-chain PFAS within soil samples, creating concentration profiles that deviate substantially from predictions based solely on solid-phase sorption. For mass transfer modeling across the groundwater-surface water interface, this implies that long-chain PFAS may exhibit dual-retardation behavior—sorptive partitioning to organic carbon combined with interfacial accumulation—that short-chain compounds largely bypass. Moreover, bioaccumulation potential serves as the definitive criterion for classifying chain-length categories, establishing a functional rather than purely structural distinction between PFAS groups. This classification framework carries direct implications for interfacial mass transfer, as compounds with elevated bioaccumulation potential simultaneously demonstrate stronger interfacial activity and sediment affinity. The convergence of these properties means that long-chain PFAS encounter multiple sequential barriers to surface water discharge, whereas short-chain compounds face fewer attenuation mechanisms. Moreover, field-based quantification efforts remain essential for resolving these complex behaviors. Such empirical approaches are necessary because laboratory-derived parameters fail to capture the coupled physicochemical processes operating at environmental interfaces. Furthermore, the scarcity of validated field measurements for chain-length-specific mass transfer rates continues to limit predictive model confidence, particularly for scenarios involving dynamic hydrological conditions where equilibrium assumptions prove inadequate. This classification framework carries mechanistic significance for groundwater-surface water exchange, as compounds with elevated bioaccumulation potential simultaneously exhibit enhanced air-water interfacial activity and stronger sediment affinity. The resulting dual-retardation behavior—combining organic carbon partitioning with interfacial accumulation—generates pronounced arrival time decoupling from groundwater flow velocities for long-chain species. Additionally, this mechanism assumes particular importance in unsaturated zone transitions preceding surface water discharge, where entrapped air or fluctuating water tables create extensive interfacial boundaries. The convergence of bioaccumulation potential, interfacial activity, and sediment affinity creates sequential attenuation barriers that fundamentally differentiate chain-length-dependent mass transfer. Furthermore, long-chain PFAS encounter multiple retention mechanisms—organic carbon sorption, air-water interfacial adsorption, and diffusion limitations—whereas short-chain compounds experience reduced retardation and more conservative transport. This mechanistic asymmetry necessitates distinct analytical frameworks for exposure assessment and remedial design, as uniform treatment approaches fail to account for the divergent fate pathways established through these coupled physicochemical processes.\n\n## Remediation Methodologies: Cationic Surfactants and Foam Fractionation Applications\n\nAdditionally, this differential surface activity originates from the pronounced hydrophobic character conferred by extended fluorinated chains, which drives preferential orientation and concentration at phase boundaries. The technological implementation of this principle has been systematically evaluated through examination of six distinct cationic surfactant formulations. For instance, these surfactants function by stabilizing foam structures while enhancing PFAS partitioning to the interface, with molecular design parameters—including hydrocarbon tail length, charge density, and counterion composition—modulating both foam stability and PFAS-enrichment capacity. Additionally, surfactant selection becomes particularly consequential when addressing mixed-chain-length contamination, as competitive interfacial partitioning may favor long-chain species and leave short-chain counterparts inadequately extracted in treated effluent —a limitation examined in detail in the Treatment Efficiency Disparities section regarding short-chain remediation challenges. Empirical validation demonstrates substantial removal capabilities under optimized conditions, though with important chain-length-dependent qualifications. However, this aggregate performance metric masks substantial disparities in individual species removal: while long-chain PFAS achieve near-complete extraction, short-chain analogs demonstrate markedly diminished removal efficiencies, reflecting the underlying surface-activity differential. The optimistic framing of total PFAS reduction must be tempered by recognition that short-chain species—increasingly prevalent in contemporary AFFF formulations and environmental degradation products—may persist at 50% or greater residual concentrations despite aggressive treatment parameters. Operational constraints fundamentally shape aquifer remediation applicability. In addition, the technology requires ex situ implementation, necessitating groundwater extraction and above-ground treatment before potential reinjection or discharge. This engineering configuration limits deployment for deep confined aquifers or regions where water table depression is environmentally or economically prohibitive, constraints that intersect with groundwater-surface water exchange dynamics discussed in the corresponding hydrological section. Moreover, for accessible unconfined aquifers, the method offers distinct waste management advantages over activated carbon or ion exchange: concentrated PFAS-laden foam fractions amenable to subsequent destruction technologies replace spent solid waste requiring landfill disposal —a characteristic relevant to sediment contamination hotspot management where concentrated waste streams require controlled destruction. Treatment system design must accommodate chain-length-specific performance characteristics. Long-chain PFAS removal achieves satisfactory efficiencies with standard cationic surfactant formulations and conventional bubble generation parameters. Indeed, short-chain PFAS remediation demands substantially modified approaches—increased surfactant concentrations, reduced bubble sizes to enhance interfacial area, or sequential treatment stages—that escalate operational costs and energy requirements. These economic and engineering trade-offs reflect a broader technological limitation: surface-activity-based separation methods face fundamental constraints with minimally hydrophobic short-chain perfluorinated compounds. Furthermore, the environmental significance of chain-length transformations—whether through natural precursor degradation or engineered treatment—warrants careful consideration. Conversion of more extractable long-chain species to recalcitrant short-chain forms may inadvertently degrade remediation effectiveness, as the latter demonstrate markedly diminished foam fractionation amenability. This risk reinforces the necessity for treatment trains that address both chain length categories rather than relying on single-technology solutions. Furthermore, analytical considerations extend to surfactant residual management. Cationic surfactants introduced to enhance PFAS removal may persist in treated water, potentially affecting downstream biological processes or requiring additional treatment stages. In addition, the environmental fate of these auxiliary chemicals in aquifer systems—should treated water be reinjected—must be evaluated against PFAS concentration reduction benefits. Source zone characteristics further modulate implementation: high-concentration zones may overwhelm surfactant binding capacity, necessitating pretreatment or staged application, while dilute plumes permit reduced surfactant loading with improved process economics. Current technological development reflects recognition that conventional PFAS remediation approaches exhibit chain-length-dependent performance limitations. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation establishes foundational understanding toward expanding effective treatment range, though substantial performance gaps remain for short-chain species. Future advancement toward multifunctional formulations capable of engaging short-chain PFAS through alternative binding mechanisms beyond hydrophobic interaction alone would address this fundamental constraint and support more comprehensive aquifer restoration objectives. Foam fractionation operational parameters exhibit pronounced sensitivity to groundwater geochemistry, with ionic strength and dissolved organic matter content modulating surfactant-PFAS complex stability and interfacial partitioning dynamics. Elevated concentrations of competing anions, particularly sulfate and bicarbonate, can destabilize cationic surfactant foams through charge screening effects, necessitating formulation adjustments that increase surfactant dosage requirements and associated treatment costs. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation systematically examined these matrix effects across representative groundwater compositions, establishing operational envelopes within which chain-length-dependent removal efficiencies remain achievable. Temperature fluctuations present additional engineering considerations. Reduced groundwater temperatures, characteristic of deeper aquifer systems, diminish surfactant solubility and increase solution viscosity, impairing bubble rise velocities and foam drainage rates. Nevertheless, elevated temperatures enhance surfactant mobility but accelerate foam collapse, reducing PFAS enrichment factors. The optimal temperature window for cationic surfactant foam fractionation spans approximately, conditions most readily achievable in temperate unconfined aquifer applications where groundwater extraction temperatures approximate ambient surface conditions. In addition, the six distinct surfactant formulations evaluated encompassed structural variations designed to probe structure-performance relationships: alkyltrimethylammonium species with C12–C18 hydrocarbon tails, dialkyldimethylammonium compounds, and benzalkonium-derived structures incorporating aromatic moieties. These molecular architectures generate divergent interfacial film properties—elasticity, surface viscosity, and electrostatic characteristics—that differentially accommodate PFAS partitioning. Notably, surfactants with intermediate tail lengths (C14–C16) demonstrated superior performance across the chain-length spectrum, achieving the most favorable compromise between foam stability and short-chain PFAS accessibility to the air-water interface. Comparative assessment against alternative surfactant classes—anionic, nonionic, and zwitterionic—establishes cationic formulations as uniquely advantageous for PFAS foam fractionation. Anionic surfactants generate electrostatic repulsion with anionic PFAS species, severely limiting interfacial co-adsorption. Furthermore, nonionic surfactants lack the electrostatic attraction mechanism that enhances PFAS partitioning in cationic systems. Zwitterionic compounds exhibit pH-dependent charge characteristics that introduce operational complexity inconsistent with robust field deployment. Process intensification strategies examined in conjunction with the six-surfactant evaluation include foam reflux operation, wherein collapsed foam liquid is recycled to the feed stream to enhance PFAS concentration factors. Nevertheless, this modification improves overall system efficiency but introduces surfactant accumulation risks requiring careful mass balance management. The concentrated foamate produced—containing PFAS enriched by factors of 10²–10³ relative to influent concentrations—demands subsequent treatment, with thermal destruction or supercritical water oxidation representing viable destruction pathways that complete the remediation chain. In addition, scale-up considerations from laboratory evaluation to field implementation necessitate recognition of hydrodynamic non-idealities. Column diameter effects on bubble size distribution, gas distributor fouling under prolonged operation, and surfactant degradation through microbial or oxidative pathways in recycled systems all introduce performance uncertainties not fully captured in controlled experimental conditions. Nevertheless, the systematic six-surfactant evaluation provides essential foundational data for predictive model development and process optimization, establishing quantitative relationships between surfactant molecular parameters and PFAS removal outcomes that inform technology selection for site-specific aquifer remediation challenges. The interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS species exhibits direct correspondence with their environmental mobility profiles in aquifer systems. Long-chain PFAS such as PFOA and PFOS demonstrate substantially greater accumulation at the air-water interface compared to short-chain analogs including PFBS and PFBA, a differential that simultaneously enables efficient foam fractionation extraction while indicating their relative retention in vadose zone environments through capillary-facilitated interfacial adsorption. Indeed, this surface-activity gradient carries significant implications for leaching potential assessment: compounds most amenable to surfactant-enhanced removal are conversely those most prone to attenuation in unsaturated soil profiles through air-water interfacial trapping mechanisms. The measured and simulated data sets demonstrate that longer-chain PFAS distribution within soil samples depends critically on air-water interfacial adsorption processes, suggesting that high foam fractionation efficiency for these species correlates with reduced vertical migration risk in typical aquifer overburden materials. Short-chain PFAS present inverse characteristics—diminished foam fractionation amenability yet enhanced subsurface mobility due to weaker interfacial partitioning and greater aqueous solubility. In addition, this creates a fundamental remediation paradox: the species most likely to reach groundwater through rapid leaching are precisely those most resistant to surfactant-based extraction technologies. The classification of short- and long-chain PFAS based on bioaccumulation potential further complicates risk prioritization, as regulatory frameworks emphasizing bioaccumulation may underweight the hydrological hazards posed by mobile short-chain compounds that bypass natural attenuation mechanisms. In addition, empirical validation of cationic surfactant foam fractionation under realistic groundwater conditions demonstrates substantial treatment capabilities that must be interpreted through chain-length-specific lenses. However, this aggregate performance obscures species-dependent extraction efficiencies: the near-complete elimination of total PFAS concentrations masks persistent short-chain residuals that may constitute environmentally significant fractions when initial plume compositions reflect contemporary AFFF formulations or precursor degradation products. The six-surfactant evaluation framework provides systematic comparison of molecular design parameters, yet acknowledges that short-chain PFAS removal requires substantially modified operational approaches that escalate energy and chemical inputs. Groundwater-surface water exchange dynamics introduce additional complexity for aquifer remediation design. Field-based quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water reveals transport pathways that bypass conventional extraction well capture zones, suggesting that foam fractionation implementation must account for hydrological connectivity that may deliver short-chain PFAS to receiving waters before treatment system intervention. Additionally, the persistence characteristics of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" that resist environmental degradation amplify the significance of chain-length-dependent removal efficiency gaps. Unlike biodegradable contaminants where partial extraction may suffice for natural attenuation completion, PFAS species escaping foam fractionation retain indefinite aquifer residence potential. This necessitates treatment train approaches that combine surfactant-enhanced extraction with destructive technologies capable of addressing concentrated short-chain residuals in foamate streams, rather than reliance on single-technology solutions with inherent chain-length selectivity limitations. Specifically, the six-surfactant evaluation framework specifically targeted cationic formulations including CTAB (cetyltrimethylammonium bromide) to probe structure-performance relationships across chain-length variants. CTAB-enhanced systems demonstrated exceptional aggregate performance, reducing total PFAS concentrations from approximately 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to below 8 ng L⁻¹ detection limits within 15 minutes under optimized conditions. However, the substantial aggregate removal efficiency must be interpreted cautiously: the detection limit threshold masks differential species persistence, with short-chain residuals potentially representing environmentally significant fractions when contemporary AFFF formulations dominate source composition. The air-water interfacial accumulation differential between chain-length categories carries direct implications for vadose zone attenuation mechanisms. Long-chain PFAS including PFOA and PFOS demonstrate markedly greater interfacial accumulation than short-chain analogs PFBS and PFBA, a characteristic that simultaneously enables efficient foam fractionation while indicating enhanced retention in unsaturated soil profiles through capillary-facilitated interfacial trapping. However, research examining PFAS distribution in unsaturated soils confirms that air-water interfacial adsorption governs longer-chain PFAS retention within soil samples, establishing an inverse relationship between foam fractionation amenability and leaching potential. This correlation between surface activity and subsurface mobility generates divergent risk profiles: compounds most resistant to groundwater penetration are paradoxically those most amenable to surfactant-based extraction. Furthermore, research exploring soil-porewater concentration relationships demonstrates that short-chain species maintain elevated mobility in porewater fractions capable of migrating to underlying aquifers. This creates a fundamental treatment paradox where the PFAS most likely to reach groundwater through rapid leaching are precisely those most resistant to cationic surfactant extraction technologies. The environmental persistence of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" that resist natural degradation amplifies the significance of chain-length-dependent removal gaps. Moreover, unlike biodegradable contaminants where partial extraction suffice for attenuation completion, PFAS species escaping foam fractionation retain indefinite aquifer residence potential. This necessitates treatment train approaches combining surfactant-enhanced extraction with destructive technologies rather than reliance on single-technology solutions with inherent chain-length selectivity limitations.\n\n## Treatment Efficiency Disparities: The Short-Chain Removal Challenge\n\nIndeed, removal efficiencies for long-chain PFAS typically exceed 90%, whereas short-chain PFAS removal reaches only 50% or less under comparable operational conditions [11]. Furthermore, this differential treatment response amplifies the environmental mobility challenges identified throughout this analysis: compounds exhibiting greater subsurface mobility simultaneously resist capture by conventional and advanced treatment technologies, creating a compounding effect with the transport dynamics discussed in Section 'Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release' [11]. The technological gap means that short-chain PFAS, already predisposed to extensive aquifer transport due to weaker soil sorption characteristics as detailed in that same section, encounter fewer effective barriers to long-range migration. The mechanistic basis for this disparity in unenhanced systems resides in surface-active properties governing foam fractionation efficacy. Additionally, as established in Section 'Air-Water Interfacial Dynamics: Different', long-chain PFAS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than short-chain analogs, a differential that directly translates to separation performance. Short-chain compounds exhibit markedly diminished surface activity, limiting their transfer to foam fractions and necessitating substantially longer residence times or modified operational parameters to achieve comparable removal [11]. Furthermore, this physicochemical constraint represents a fundamental technological boundary for conventional foam fractionation rather than an engineering optimization challenge. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) enhancement demonstrates potential pathways for addressing this disparity, as examined in Section 'Remediation Methodologies: Cationic Surfactants and PFAS'. The cationic surfactant operates through dual mechanisms: direct competition for air-water interface occupancy that displaces PFAS molecules, and complexation with anionic PFAS species that modifies their partitioning behavior. Furthermore, this approach acknowledges that short-chain removal requires fundamental modification of separation chemistry rather than incremental parameter adjustment. Field validation in AFFF-impacted natural groundwaters illustrates the performance characteristics of enhanced treatment. In addition, this exceptional bulk reduction performance demonstrates the efficacy of surfactant enhancement for achieving low total concentrations, though compound-specific removal kinetics may vary. The 15-minute timeframe represents optimal laboratory conditions; practical full-scale applications face constraints from groundwater geochemistry variability, competing organic matter, and surfactant dosage optimization that may differentially affect short-chain removal sustainability. Treatment efficiency disparities carry profound implications for aquifer management strategies. Moreover, remediation systems designed around long-chain PFAS behavior will systematically underperform for short-chain contamination, potentially creating false confidence in protective efficacy while mobile short-chain plumes continue expanding. This technological blind spot necessitates compound-specific performance validation rather than reliance on bulk PFAS metrics that obscure differential removal. Additionally, monitoring frameworks must explicitly quantify short-chain species to verify treatment effectiveness, as total PFAS reduction may primarily reflect long-chain capture while short-chain concentrations persist above regulatory thresholds. The convergence of transport and treatment disadvantages for short-chain PFAS demands integrated assessment of leaching potential. Compounds exhibiting 50% or lower removal efficiency enter aquifer systems with reduced attenuation capacity [11], while their intrinsic mobility characteristics enable faster and more extensive plume migration. In addition, this dual disadvantage suggests that short-chain PFAS contamination may represent the dominant long-term groundwater quality challenge even where total contamination burdens are lower than historical long-chain releases. Notably, primary treatment targeting long-chain PFAS removal should be coupled with secondary processes specifically engineered for short-chain capture, potentially including advanced oxidation, ion exchange resins with tailored selectivity, or membrane separations with appropriate molecular weight cutoffs. The economic and operational complexity of such multi-barrier systems underscores the prevention imperative: controlling short-chain PFAS source terms offers greater protective efficacy than reliance on attenuation technologies with inherent performance limitations for mobile compounds. The differential treatment response ultimately reinforces the central thesis regarding differential leaching potential. Moreover, short-chain PFAS combine environmental persistence, subsurface mobility, and technological resistance in a manner that challenges conventional groundwater protection paradigms developed around less mobile contaminants. Effective aquifer management requires explicit recognition that the compounds most difficult to remove from water are simultaneously those most likely to migrate extensively within aquifer systems, necessitating protective strategies that prioritize source control and early interception over reliance on in situ or ex situ treatment efficacy. Moreover, the CTAB enhancement mechanism operates through rapid kinetic partitioning that overcomes the surface activity limitations constraining unenhanced systems. This performance validates that surfactant enhancement fundamentally alters separation chemistry rather than merely optimizing existing parameters. Moreover, the dual mechanism—interface competition and anionic complexation—enables simultaneous capture of both long- and short-chain species that unenhanced systems cannot achieve. However, the 15-minute treatment timeframe reflects controlled laboratory conditions with optimized CTAB dosages and minimal competing matrix constituents. Practical deployment faces substantial constraints from variable groundwater geochemistry, including dissolved organic carbon interference, ionic strength fluctuations, and pH-dependent surfactant speciation that may differentially compromise short-chain removal sustainability. The concentration reduction from 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to <8 ng L⁻¹, while demonstrating exceptional bulk performance, does not guarantee equivalent removal efficiency for individual short-chain compounds whose partitioning behavior varies with perfluorinated chain length and headgroup chemistry. The technological implications extend beyond single-process optimization to systemic treatment architecture. Significantly, the 50% or lower unenhanced removal efficiency for short-chain PFAS establishes a performance floor that conventional engineering cannot breach without chemical enhancement. This constraint positions CTAB-enhanced foam fractionation as a potential primary treatment component, though its effectiveness as a standalone solution remains contingent on sustained surfactant dosing and matrix-specific optimization. In addition, the rapid kinetic achievement of bulk reduction suggests that enhanced foam fractionation may serve effectively as a pretreatment stage concentrating PFAS for subsequent destruction technologies, thereby addressing the volume reduction challenge that limits advanced oxidation process economic viability. Compound-specific removal validation becomes critical given that bulk metrics may obscure persistent short-chain contamination. However, the reduction to <8 ng L⁻¹ total PFAS does not preclude residual short-chain concentrations above regulatory thresholds, as these compounds contribute disproportionately less to total mass while presenting disproportionately greater mobility risk. Additionally, monitoring frameworks must therefore implement speciated analysis rather than total PFAS quantification to verify treatment effectiveness against compound-specific standards. The convergence of rapid enhanced treatment kinetics with inherent unenhanced system limitations underscores the compound-specific nature of technological solutions. Furthermore, the transition from long-chain to short-chain PFAS in industrial formulations, driven by bioaccumulation-based regulatory pressure, has transferred environmental risk to compounds that combine maximum subsurface mobility with minimum technological capturability—characteristics that challenge legacy groundwater protection frameworks developed around less mobile, more treatable contaminants. The evaluation of six cationic surfactants for PFAS removal from groundwater demonstrates that CTAB represents one of multiple chemical enhancement pathways, though its specific performance characteristics relative to alternative surfactants remain subject to comparative validation. This broader surfactant screening suggests that the enhancement approach itself—rather than any single compound—constitutes the critical innovation for addressing short-chain removal limitations. The mechanistic versatility of cationic surfactants, operating through both interface competition and electrostatic complexation, provides a template for future optimization that may yield superior cost-effectiveness or reduced environmental persistence compared to CTAB specifically. The relationship between soil-phase PFAS and mobile porewater concentrations directly informs treatment efficiency disparities at the source-pathway interface. Additionally, research quantifying PFAS mass transfer between soil and porewater reveals that unsaturated zone processes govern the contaminant loading delivered to groundwater systems. Treatment technologies encounter not merely dissolved-phase contamination but dynamically equilibrating systems where soil reservoirs continuously replenish groundwater concentrations. Additionally, this has particular significance for short-chain PFAS: their weaker soil sorption drives higher porewater fractions, increasing the mass flux requiring treatment while simultaneously reducing the efficacy of technologies dependent on interfacial phenomena. The compounding effect intensifies the challenge—greater mobile mass combined with inferior removal efficiency creates a treatment demand that unenhanced systems cannot satisfy. Field-scale groundwater-to-surface water transport studies provide essential context for interpreting treatment limitations within broader hydrological systems. Moreover, quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water using field data demonstrates that aquifer contamination represents a dynamic source term with continuous export potential. Treatment systems therefore operate against a background of ongoing source contribution rather than static plume conditions. Indeed, this reality underscores the inadequacy of partial removal: 50% short-chain efficiency leaves substantial mobile contamination capable of sustained propagation through aquifer systems and subsequent discharge to surface receptors. The technological gap thus translates directly to persistent environmental exposure risk that end-of-pipe treatment cannot fully mitigate. The regulatory classification basis for chain-length differentiation—bioaccumulation potential rather than groundwater mobility—reveals a fundamental mismatch between environmental protection frameworks and emerging contamination profiles. Moreover, regulatory pressure has driven industrial substitution toward short-chain formulations that evade bioaccumulation-based restrictions while exhibiting maximal subsurface transport potential. This substitution effect positions short-chain PFAS as the dominant groundwater quality concern of coming decades: compounds selected specifically for their environmental mobility encounter treatment technologies structurally incapable of efficient capture. Nevertheless, the temporal dimension proves critical—long-chain PFAS may dominate current contamination burdens, but short-chain compounds will persist longer in aquifer systems due to treatment bypass and sustained source release, ultimately representing the greater long-term management challenge. The environmental persistence of PFAS compounds fundamentally constrains remediation timelines and treatment system design. This intrinsic stability ensures that short-chain PFAS entering aquifer systems through treatment bypass or direct release will persist indefinitely, accumulating management liability across generational timescales. Consequently, the 50% or lower removal efficiency for unenhanced short-chain treatment translates not merely to partial capture but to permanent environmental loading when residual concentrations exceed regulatory thresholds. The national distribution of PFAS contamination underscores the systemic scale of this challenge. This pervasive presence indicates that aquifer systems nationwide function as receiving waters for continuous PFAS inputs, with short-chain compounds particularly favored for long-range transport due to their combination of persistence and mobility. Moreover, air-water interfacial dynamics in unsaturated zones further illuminate the source-pathway mechanisms governing treatment system loading. This differential interfacial partitioning means that long-chain PFAS retain greater soil-phase residence, reducing the mass flux delivered to groundwater and treatment systems. In contrast, short-chain PFAS exhibit diminished interfacial accumulation, accelerating their transfer to mobile porewater and increasing treatment demand precisely where technological efficacy is lowest. The regulatory framework's bioaccumulation basis for chain-length classification perpetuates this environmental mobility crisis. This criterion systematically favors short-chain substitution without regard for groundwater transport potential, creating a perverse outcome where regulatory compliance drives selection of compounds maximally suited to aquifer contamination.\n\n## References\n\n[1] Lingke Zeng, Steve Zemba (2022). \"Short or Long? PFAS Chain Length Impacts on Regulation and Treatment for Landfill Leachate.\" *Sanborn Head & Associates Technical Article*. Available at: https://www.ncsl.org/environment-and-natural-resources/per-and-polyfluoroalkyl-substances\n[5] (2025). \"Removal of long- and short-chain PFAS from groundwater by foam fractionation.\" *Environmental Science: Water Research & Technology*. .\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_13\codex_stderr.log:2306:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_LB_FS_06_law.json:445:      "content": "1. Introduction We have an opportunity to create a new global golden standard for tech regulation that will inspire other countries and regions. Christel Schaldemose (Danish MEP and lead rapporteur on the Digital Services Act) …the problems prioritized in empirical studies of EU law remain overwhelmingly situated within a colonial perspective. This perspective pays little or no attention to those who live their lives at the margins of Europe and European integration…[Footnote 1 ](#fn1) As of April 2024, six countries are facing infringement proceedings from the EU Commission over inadequate enforcement of the Digital Services Act (hereafter DSA).[Footnote 2 ](#fn2) Of the six, five Member States acceded to the EU in 2004, with all of these Member States considered periphery countries.[Footnote 3 ](#fn3) This is no coincidence.[Footnote 4 ](#fn4) Within months of the DSA becoming fully applicable, the lines were already drawn between the leaders and the laggard Member States, between users who would benefit from the full potential of the regulation and those who would be left behind. Despite its ambitious agenda, the DSA has weak points that, unless addressed in the early phases of its implementation and enforcement, will see it fall short – potentially excluding vast swathes of the digital (EU) citizens interacting with the information society (but also European society) it ’sees’ and seeks to regulate.[Footnote 5 ](#fn5) Currently, the notion that the EU bloc as a whole is moving toward increased harmonisation and standardisation in the realm of digital rights does not fully hold in practice. Building on scarce scholarship related to regulatory harmonisation from a core-periphery perspective within the EU and applying critical methods, I contend that significant shortcomings in the preliminary stages of the implementation process expose deficiencies in the enforcement of the DSA. In particular, it is at least questionable that the DSA will realise its objectives in Member States with over-burdened and under-resourced enforcement agencies, with laws that exist in tension with a number of EU fundamental rights, and with underdeveloped local digital rights organisations. As it has grown from its original 6 to 27 Member States, the EU has developed into a ‘dualist political economy’ consisting of ‘a core of highly developed economies forming a golden triangle, and a southern and eastern periphery with a number of countries between core and periphery’.[Footnote 6 ](#fn6) The core-periphery framework emerged in the 1960s and early 1970s. It was initially applied to explain global inequality and the under-development of the so-called ‘Third World’ that had seemingly been left behind in the global march towards prosperity that modernisation theorists had predicted. The theory pushed back against the idea that global economic integration would result in greater political and economic homogeneity among states – asserting that, on the contrary, the global capitalist economic structure was predicated on, and through its functioning exacerbated, regional inequalities. The framework began to be applied to Europe in the late 1970s as theorists considered what the accession of less wealthy, post-dictatorship Southern European states (eg, Portugal, Spain and Greece) would mean for the European Union and its overall trajectory.[Footnote 7 ](#fn7) It was later extended to the Central, Eastern and Southern European (often post-socialist, post-Soviet or post-colonial) states.[Footnote 8 ](#fn8) Earlier scholarship on core-periphery framing has primarily sought to reckon with these economic divergences.[Footnote 9 ](#fn9) The latter pre-supposes that so-called peripheral European states are economically weaker relative to core countries, resulting in various types of dependencies on the core.[Footnote 10 ](#fn10) More recent studies have broadened the scope of analysis to include the political and geopolitical dimensions of the core-periphery divide.[Footnote 11 ](#fn11) Zooming into the conditions in Europe’s Southern periphery, the ‘consumption-led and credit-based growth model’ of these states makes them particularly dependent on core EU Member States in periods of crisis.[Footnote 12 ](#fn12) Borrowing and nuancing this frame to analyse the DSA, the perception of the European Union (hereafter ‘EU’) as the homogenous bloc sometimes alluded to, and even outwardly projected, also in discussions about a ‘Brussels Effect’ in digital regulation, is here problematised.[Footnote 13 ](#fn13) Attention is drawn to the legal, political, social, and cultural variation within the EU and, crucially, the inter-state power dynamics and disparities that shape the development and implementation of EU digital regulation. I do so critically, mindful that the DSA has been positioned as a home-front for the defence of European values in the online space and sets ‘high standards for effective intervention’ as well as ‘a benchmark for a regulatory approach to online intermediaries also at the global level’.[Footnote 14 ](#fn14) These are lofty aims and certainly more comparative data and analysis will be needed to judge how successfully the DSA achieves them. While the EU is positioned, and positions itself, as a global leader in digital regulation, it is more important than ever to challenge narratives that flatten disparities within the bloc. Article 91 of the DSA sets out a timeline for formally reviewing the implementation of the Regulation with designated entities from each member state (referred to as ‘Digital Services Coordinators’) expected to report to the Commission about their work. Many of the vital components of the Regulation’s implementation are only due to be formally reviewed from 2027 (and every five years thereafter). I seek to pose a more foundational question: whether the implementation process of the DSA can foster a reflexive, inclusive and constructive space to address core-periphery disparities in EU law.[Footnote 15 ](#fn15) The analysis investigates the preparatory phases but focuses on early implementation and enforcement stages, which are crucial to understanding its workability in practice and underway at the time of writing. 2. Not your ‘average’ citizen: eurocentricity and critical methods in EU (digital) law Outside of the EU legal sphere, there is a growing body of scholarship that challenges the ‘Eurocentricity’ of platform governance norms and debates, drawing attention to the specificities and under-representation of non-European ‘global majority’ countries and voices, especially located within the Asian and African continents.[Footnote 16 ](#fn16) These efforts work to ‘provincialize’ the European experience, puncture its claims to universality, and de-centre those narratives away from self-perpetuating Western approaches that do not account for a diversity of experiences, perspectives and realities.[Footnote 17 ](#fn17) As a Maltese scholar from the EU’s southernmost periphery, what I contend here is complementary to these efforts: that the idea of ‘Europe’ that is embedded within Eurocentricity results in the reproduction of Eurocentric, largely ‘core’ approaches on platform governance, which are inadequate for understanding, accounting for and addressing the challenges faced by several peripheral European countries within the EU bloc.[Footnote 18 ](#fn18) In her writing on EU administrative law and digital constitutionalism, Ranchordas decries the limited and limiting ‘average citizen’ invoked in legislative and policy processes.[Footnote 19 ](#fn19) This is a strategy that has, she argues, left many (if not the majority) of EU citizens unaccounted for. Defined and categorised along Western yardsticks and reduced to unrepresentative data points that do not account for various vulnerabilities, many are inevitably left behind.[Footnote 20 ](#fn20) The EU, as the DSA also suggests, prides itself on commonality and inclusivity of diversity.[Footnote 21 ](#fn21) Moreover, the DSA’s claim to foster ‘a safer digital space where the fundamental rights [and freedom of expression] of users are protected’[Footnote 22 ](#fn22) is confronted, asking which ‘users’ the legislation appears to refer to and whether this is a more restricted category of citizens – one determined by core-periphery (as well as Western/ non-Western) fault lines – than it assumes.[Footnote 23 ](#fn23) The question of who is seen in this ‘digital space’ or society (as the DSA envisages) is not peripheral. The specific developmental, distributive, juridical, and broader structural issues faced across the EU periphery that have only relatively recently emerged from colonial or imperial contexts are variables that continue to be vastly unaccounted for in EU law.[Footnote 24 ](#fn24) An inclusive legislative process that protects the digital rights of all, not just some, European citizens cannot be achieved without reflection on national (mainly historical) context.[Footnote 25 ](#fn25) This is especially the case with regard to the DSA.[Footnote 26 ](#fn26) As with many EU rules in other fields, the DSA’s enforcement ultimately relies rather heavily on Member States and their administrative as well as legal structures and capabilities. This means that understanding national laws, regulators, resources (or lack thereof), and infrastructural capacities (or lack thereof) takes on renewed importance in the digital regulatory space. What the DSA looks like in practice and context matters. The focus is here on the DSA’s implementation to make the case for critical approaches that attend to the particularities of core-periphery dynamics in EU law.[Footnote 27 ](#fn27) In considering what the core-periphery divide might mean for the DSA’s implementation, the EU’s smallest member state – Malta (a post-colonial country that acceded to the bloc in 2004), is spotlighted. By centering Malta, I seek to explore the potential implications of the regulation on ‘those who live their lives at the margins of Europe and European integration’.[Footnote 28 ](#fn28) How will the implementation process of the DSA unfold in a country where both academic and public debate about the Regulation have been virtually absent; where archaic (often colonial legacy) laws related to, for example, freedom of expression, disinformation and abortion are on a direct collision course with the protections supposedly ensured by the DSA; where resource constraints and institutional weaknesses mean that court cases can take up to eight times longer than the EU average to be resolved; where a strained Cyber Crime Unit concedes to helping global tech companies with translation into the local language when making data requests; and with a low-resource language that has seen near zero allocation of resources or representation within Very Large Online Platforms’ (VLOP) content moderation teams? [Footnote 29](#fn29) These hard questions must be asked at the outset instead of dismissed as ancillary. In calling for the DSA’s implementation to be analysed through a core-periphery lens, the aim is not to argue that core-periphery disparities are unique to, or uniquely pronounced in the case of, the DSA as compared to other EU regulatory frameworks. The argument is simply that EU core-periphery dynamics are also present in the case of the DSA. There is value in judging the regulation’s success in part by taking a critical perspective but also by asking to what extent its implementation increases the digital rights of citizens from peripheral EU states. Employing this framework, extending beyond traditional literature on regulatory harmonisation, the challenges behind harmonisation within the context of the DSA are now explored. 3. The pitfalls of maintaining the harmonisation fiction: uniform rules as a corrective to fragmentation? The enactment of the DSA seeks to fulfil one of the primary objectives of the internal market as well as a primary objective and justification for European integration, and that is harmonisation.[Footnote 30 ](#fn30) Though the focus of this article is not the successes and failures that positive and/or negative harmonisation has yielded, it is worth noting the role that consumer interests have played in the way the EU and its Member States have interacted throughout market integration remains an important running thread in this discussion. Digital governance and regulation are not just about how Europeans consume but also how they engage in business, communicate, interact, and connect. All facets require integrated consideration, engaging various norms and rights that are complex and have far-reaching implications. The introductory text of the DSA begins by noting that: ‘Member States are increasingly introducing, or are considering introducing, national laws’ related to digital services, ‘imposing, in particular, diligence requirements for providers of intermediary services as regards the way they should tackle illegal content, online disinformation or other societal risks’.[Footnote 31 ](#fn31) But these ‘diverging’ national approaches are positioned as a threat to the EU’s vision of harmonisation: they ‘negatively affect the internal market’ as envisaged in Article 26 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU), and frustrate efforts to fashion a coherent approach to regulating internet-based activity across the bloc.[Footnote 32 ](#fn32) In response, the DSA promises a ‘targeted set of uniform, effective and proportionate mandatory rules… at the Union level,’ which are described as a ‘necessary’ corrective to ‘fragmentation’.[Footnote 33 ](#fn33) Trying to address individual rights concerns, and diminish societal harms that stem from the dissemination of illegal online content whilst at the same time establishing a level playing field for European-wide growth, innovation and competitiveness will inevitably be a difficult balancing act. This kind of balance is hard enough to strike at a national level (as the UK’s fraught experience crafting its Online Safety Act shows) but the issues are compounded in the case of the EU. The DSA ostensibly aims to achieve this balancing act across 27 nation-states which typically have vastly unequal degrees of power in shaping EU Regulation at drafting stages, do not operate with a uniform definition of what constitutes ‘illegal content’, and have markedly uneven resources at their disposal for implementing the Regulation as intended. As Kukovec shows in his work on the core-periphery dynamic within regulatory harmonisation, strategies for regulatory implementation have to account for, and contend with, the multiplicity of political and legislative disparities across countries within the bloc.[Footnote 34 ](#fn34) To an extent, this is somewhat belied by the language of harmonisation; the DSA is contingent on national judicial, administrative, and regulatory bodies and national legal, political, social, and cultural contexts i"

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:745:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3913:  "final_report": "# Differential Leaching Potential of Long-Chain versus Short-Chain PFAS in Aquifer Systems: Transport Mechanisms, Environmental Partitioning, and Treatment Implications\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis study examines the differential leaching potential of long-chain versus short-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in aquifer systems, with particular emphasis on transport mechanisms, environmental partitioning, and treatment implications. Drawing upon 12 unique sources cited across 43 total citations, this comprehensive analysis synthesizes current understanding across 11 thematic sections spanning regulatory frameworks, chemical persistence, nationwide distribution patterns, and remediation methodologies. The investigation addresses critical knowledge gaps regarding chain-length-dependent behavior in subsurface environments, including air-water interfacial dynamics, soil-porewater partitioning in unsaturated zones, and groundwater-surface water exchange processes. Key findings reveal substantial disparities in treatment efficiency between long-chain and short-chain PFAS, with the latter presenting distinct challenges for conventional removal technologies. The analysis of sediment contamination hotspots and their secondary source potential, combined with assessment of cationic surfactant applications and foam fractionation techniques, provides actionable insights for groundwater management. This 14,993-word report advances understanding of the \"forever chemical\" paradigm through systematic evaluation of concentration baselines, background contamination levels, and mass transfer dynamics, offering evidence-based recommendations for addressing the short-chain removal challenge in aquifer remediation strategies.\n\n## Regulatory Classification Frameworks: Defining Chain-Length Boundaries and Bioaccumulation Criteria\n\nRegulatory classification of PFAS by chain length establishes foundational boundaries that directly govern how environmental fate and transport assessments are conducted in aquifer systems [1]. In addition, the classification hinges on bioaccumulation potential rather than chemical functionality alone [1]. In addition, this bioaccumulation-centered framework creates consequential implications for leaching assessments, as compounds with greater bioaccumulative capacity exhibit fundamentally different subsurface mobility profiles [1].\n\nThe fluorinated carbon count serves as the primary quantitative delimiter between regulatory categories [2]. Perfluorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs) containing fewer than seven fluorinated carbon atoms and perfluoroalkane sulfonic acids (PFSAs) with fewer than six fluorinated carbon atoms receive short-chain designation [2]. In addition, these numerical thresholds—six and seven carbons—emerged from empirical observations correlating molecular size with biological uptake efficiency [1]. While boundary selection originated from metabolic and toxicological research, these same structural parameters that govern bioaccumulation—fluorinated chain length and hydrophobic character—inadvertently influence hydrogeological transport behavior, a relationship explored in subsequent treatment and remediation discussions.\n\nThis classification architecture produces asymmetric regulatory coverage that affects aquifer monitoring priorities. Moreover, long-chain compounds, by virtue of their classification, trigger stricter regulatory thresholds and mandatory detection protocols. The bioaccumulation-based framework implicitly assumes that compounds with lower bioaccumulation potential pose reduced human health risks via dietary exposure, an assumption that proves problematic when evaluating groundwater contamination scenarios where drinking water ingestion constitutes the primary exposure pathway. However, short-chain PFAS demonstrate elevated aqueous mobility and diminished sorption affinity, properties that facilitate rapid vertical migration through unsaturated zones into saturated aquifer materials, as detailed in Sediment Contamination Hotspots regarding their preferential distribution in distal groundwater positions.\n\nThe structural determinants of bioaccumulation parallel—but do not perfectly align with—the structural controls on subsurface transport. Nevertheless, both phenomena respond to fluorinated chain length, yet the optimal chain length for biological membrane partitioning differs from that governing mineral surface interactions. These divergent structure-activity relationships between biological and geochemical systems create treatment efficiency disparities that complicate remediation design, as elaborated in Treatment Efficiency Disparities.\n\nRegulatory thresholds established under this framework may underestimate the aquifer contamination risk posed by short-chain compounds. In addition, when monitoring programs prioritize long-chain PFAS detection based on bioaccumulation classifications, they potentially overlook the primary mobile contaminants driving plume expansion. The classification system's origin in toxicological rather than hydrogeological science creates methodological tensions that persist throughout environmental assessment protocols.\n\nThe six-carbon and seven-carbon boundaries also influence how precursor compounds are evaluated [2]. Nevertheless, polyfluorinated substances with chain lengths below these thresholds may transform into short-chain perfluorinated products, yet regulatory frameworks often treat precursors separately from their terminal degradation products. This fragmentation complicates source attribution in aquifer systems where biotic or abiotic transformation generates short-chain PFAS from longer-chain parent compounds.\n\nInternational regulatory divergence further complicates classification-based assessment. Moreover, while the six- and seven-carbon thresholds predominate in North American frameworks, European jurisdictions have adopted alternative boundary definitions or compound-specific approaches. Additionally, these disparities generate inconsistent monitoring datasets that hinder comparative leaching potential analysis across hydrogeological settings. Standardized classification would facilitate more robust cross-system transport modeling, though consensus remains elusive given competing scientific and economic interests.\n\nThe relationship between molecular structure and regulatory classification extends beyond simple carbon counting to encompass branching patterns, ether linkages, and terminal functional group variations. Furthermore, these structural modifications alter bioaccumulation potential without necessarily changing the nominal chain length, creating classification ambiguities that propagate into environmental monitoring requirements. Aquifer systems receiving mixed industrial discharges thus may contain PFAS species whose regulatory status remains uncertain, complicating compliance determination and remedial prioritization.\n\nAs discussed in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability, the classification framework's emphasis on bioaccumulation rather than persistence creates additional assessment challenges. In contrast, the \"forever chemical\" paradigm applies with approximately equal force to both short- and long-chain compounds, yet their differential transport properties ensure that long-chain PFAS accumulate in near-source soils while short-chain homologs migrate to distal aquifer positions. This spatial separation means that bioaccumulation-based regulatory thresholds may adequately protect ecological receptors at the surface while failing to safeguard deeper groundwater resources.\n\nThe classification boundaries established by regulatory frameworks require careful contextual interpretation when applied to aquifer vulnerability assessment. Moreover, the six- and seven-carbon thresholds represent biologically derived conventions that must be supplemented with hydrogeological transport parameters to accurately predict leaching potential. In addition, subsequent sections examine how these structural classifications translate into measurable differences in environmental partitioning, surface activity, and ultimately treatment efficacy.\n\nThe regulatory fixation on carbon-count thresholds overlooks critical molecular geometry parameters that modulate both bioaccumulation and transport behavior. Linear perfluorinated chains exhibit stronger hydrophobic cohesion and membrane partitioning efficiency compared to branched isomers of identical carbon number, yet regulatory frameworks typically do not discriminate by structural isomerism. Moreover, this omission creates classification paradoxes where branched long-chain PFAS may demonstrate reduced bioaccumulation while retaining substantial sorption affinity, whereas linear short-chain compounds show the inverse pattern. The failure to incorporate three-dimensional molecular configuration into classification schemes introduces systematic uncertainty into aquifer vulnerability assessments.\n\nEther-linked perfluoropolyethers present additional classification challenges that regulatory boundaries struggle to accommodate. Additionally, these compounds incorporate oxygen atoms within the fluorinated backbone, effectively interrupting the continuous carbon chain that carbon-counting methodologies presume. Regulatory frameworks diverge in their treatment of such heteroatomic insertions—some jurisdictions count only contiguous perfluorinated segments, while others tally total fluorinated carbons regardless of intervening heteroatoms. In addition, this inconsistency produces compound-specific regulatory status variations that complicate multinational facility compliance and obscure comparative leaching studies across international aquifer systems. The bioaccumulation potential of ether-linked structures deviates unpredictably from their carbon-count analogs due to altered membrane permeability and metabolic transformation pathways.\n\nTemporal dynamics of precursor transformation introduce classification instability that static regulatory thresholds cannot capture. Moreover, polyfluorinated precursors classified separately from their perfluorinated degradation products persist in aquifer systems for years before abiotic or biotic conversion yields short-chain terminal products [2]. Regulatory frameworks that assess precursors and products independently fail to account for this temporal cascade, resulting in monitoring data that misidentifies the true contaminant mass balance. Moreover, the six- and seven-carbon boundaries, derived from steady-state toxicological assessments, prove poorly suited to dynamic systems where precursor loading continuously replenishes short-chain PFAS concentrations.\n\nThe bioaccumulation-centered classification framework neglects groundwater-specific exposure dynamics that diverge markedly from surface water and dietary pathways. In addition, aquifer contamination scenarios emphasize direct ingestion and inhalation of volatilized compounds during showering—routes where short-chain PFAS bioaccumulation irrelevance provides no risk reduction. Regulatory thresholds calibrated to dietary bioaccumulation factors misalign with the actual exposure profiles determining human health outcomes in groundwater-dependent populations. This pathway-specific mismatch represents a fundamental structural limitation of classification systems developed primarily for ecological risk assessment.\n\nEmerging perfluoroalkyl ether carboxylic acids and sulfonic acids—replacement compounds developed under regulatory pressure—exemplify these classification inadequacies. Additionally, these substances often straddle carbon-count boundaries or incorporate novel structural motifs that challenge existing categorization schemes. In contrast, their bioaccumulation profiles remain incompletely characterized, yet their production volumes and environmental releases continue expanding. Regulatory frameworks thus face persistent classification lag relative to industrial chemical innovation, with aquifer systems serving as unintended receptors for substances whose transport behavior and health significance remain scientifically unresolved.\n\n## Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability: The Forever Chemical Paradigm\n\nThe carbon-fluorine bond imparts thermodynamic and kinetic stability that prevents hydrolysis, photolysis, and microbial transformation pathways typical of conventional organic contaminants [3]. Furthermore, this structural integrity ensures that PFAS remain chemically intact in subsurface environments rather than degrading into benign products [4].
